Chapter 2: BITH Successional Habitat and PCT Treatment Effect

Joint Bayesian Analysis — MBW (2012–2023) + HELP (2002–2011)

Published

May 14, 2026

1 Survey Data Overview

This chapter integrates two independent BITH survey datasets collected across New Brunswick clearcut regeneration areas:

  • MBW (Mountain Birdwatch) — passive unlimited-radius point counts, 2012–2023. Count recorded as the maximum detection within 50 m across all time periods in a survey visit.
  • HELP — standardized playback surveys, 2002–2011. Count is the maximum summed detection indicator across 10 timed intervals at a station.

Stand age covariates (TSCC: years since clearcut; TSSI: years since PCT) were re-derived for all survey stations by spatially joining station coordinates to 2026 NB Ministry of Supply and Forest stand polygons.

1.1 Survey locations

Survey station locations across New Brunswick. Blue: MBW passive point-count stations (n = 592, 94 routes, 2012–2023). Pink: HELP playback stations (n = 220, 44 routes, 2002–2011). Click a marker for station details; toggle protocols using the layer control.

1.2 Detection rates

Survey detection rates by protocol and treatment track.
Protocol Track N N_present % present Mean count (nonzero) Max count
HELP CC 1146 149 13.0 1.29 4
HELP PCT 859 122 14.2 1.34 4
MBW CC 895 30 3.4 1.60 4
MBW PCT 2618 245 9.4 1.50 6

Playback surveys (HELP) produce detection rates approximately 4–5× higher than passive counts (MBW), consistent with a protocol-level count inflation that is absorbed by the delta_HELP parameter in the joint model.

2 Stand Age Derivation

Stand age covariates were re-derived from the 2026 NB Ministry of Supply and Forest harvest shapefiles (four harvest cohorts: H1 oldest, H4 most recent) rather than relying on field-recorded values in the original survey CSVs.

The spatial join uses terra::relate("within") to assign each survey station to the stand polygon it falls inside, with a nearest-polygon fallback for stations within 50 m of a polygon boundary. For MBW, the join key is (SamplingStation, Stop) — route codes are shared across stops and a station-only join inflates rows. For HELP, station longitude values were stored as positive and required negation before projection.

The re-derivation script is rederive_mbw_tscc.R (MBW) and prepare_help_tscc.R (HELP).

Five MBW routes (15, 18, 25, 35, 75) are present in the survey data but absent from the stand-polygon layer. All 360 visit records from these routes returned no polygon match and consequently have missing Focal_TRTYR and Focal_Dist_Year values. These stations likely fall on private (non-Crown) land where the NB Ministry harvest shapefiles have no coverage. They are excluded from model fitting via the !is.na(Focal_TRTYR) filter.

Focal stand assignment for Route 66 (MBW, 2015). Coloured polygons are the NB Ministry stand polygons that fall within 50 m of a sampling station (shaded circles); colour groups polygons assigned to the same station. Grey polygons are other stands in the vicinity for spatial context. Station markers show TSCC on hover; click for full stand details.

3 LiDAR Structural Prior

The structural prior encodes the relationship between stand age and BITH predicted abundance derived from LiDAR-based stand scores across New Brunswick. Models were fit using brms with a Gamma(log) family and penalized cyclic splines (bs = "cs"). A complexity sweep was run over k = 2–10 spline basis functions for each treatment track. The parsimonious winner is the smallest k where |ΔELPD| ≤ SE(ΔELPD) — i.e., the simplest model whose LOO score is statistically indistinguishable from the best model (the best model itself satisfies this trivially at ΔELPD = 0).

3.1 Complexity sweep and k selection

CC track (TSCC): winner k = 6.

CC track LOO-ELPD by k. Bold = competitive (|ΔELPD| < SE(ΔELPD)); k = 6 selected.
k ELPD ΔELPD SE(ΔELPD) Competitive

4 1922.8 -36.6 13.9 FALSE
5 1943.0 -16.4 10.7 FALSE
6 1956.7 -2.7 6.1 TRUE
7 1950.6 -8.8 8.0 FALSE
8 1958.5 -0.9 1.4 TRUE
9 1955.6 -3.8 2.7 FALSE
10 1959.4 0.0 0.0 TRUE

PCT track (TSSI): winner k = 8.

PCT track LOO-ELPD by k. Bold = competitive (|ΔELPD| < SE(ΔELPD)); k = 8 selected.
k ELPD ΔELPD SE(ΔELPD) Competitive
2 -1565.1 -69.0 30.4 FALSE
3 -1565.1 -69.0 30.4 FALSE

10 -1496.1 0.0 0.0 TRUE

3.2 Selected prior parameters

Prior model winners: CC track k = 6; PCT track k = 8 (from production_run_2026_Balanced).

These posteriors from the LiDAR-based brms models serve as the structural prior for the joint BITH abundance model. Posterior means become the prior means for the spline coefficients and intercepts; posterior SDs set the prior widths. The prior predictive abundance curves below show the chronosequence implied by the LiDAR structural model alone, before any field survey data are considered.

Intercept prior parameters from the LiDAR structural model.
Parameter Prior mean Prior SD
Intercept (CC) -1.114 0.008
Intercept (PCT) -0.775 0.009

4 Model Specification

4.1 Likelihood

The model uses a hurdle-Poisson likelihood with protocol-specific parameters. For an observation \(y\) with structural predictor \(\mu\) and hurdle probability \(\theta\):

\[ p(y \mid \mu, \theta) = \begin{cases} \theta & y = 0 \\ (1 - \theta)\, \dfrac{e^{-\mu}\,\mu^y}{y!\,(1 - e^{-\mu})} & y > 0 \end{cases} \]

4.2 Linear predictors (four tracks)

Let \(\mathbf{x}\) denote the spline basis vector evaluated at the stand age covariate for a given observation:

\[ \log \mu = \begin{cases} \alpha_\text{cc} + \mathbf{x}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{cc} & \text{CC-MBW} \\ \alpha_\text{pct} + \mathbf{x}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{pct} & \text{PCT-MBW} \\ \alpha_\text{cc} + \delta + \mathbf{x}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{cc} & \text{CC-HELP} \\ \alpha_\text{pct} + \delta + \mathbf{x}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{pct} & \text{PCT-HELP} \end{cases} \]

where \(\delta\) (delta_HELP) is a log-scale offset absorbing the playback inflation from HELP surveys. Crucially, \(\delta\) cancels in the treatment effect:

\[ \frac{\mu_\text{pct}}{\mu_\text{cc}} = \exp\!\bigl(\alpha_\text{pct} - \alpha_\text{cc} + \mathbf{x}^\top(\boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{pct} - \boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{cc})\bigr) \]

The treatment effect ratio is therefore protocol-invariant and can be estimated jointly from both datasets.

4.3 Priors

Parameter Prior
\(\alpha_\text{cc}\), \(\alpha_\text{pct}\) \(\mathcal{N}(\hat\mu_\text{LiDAR},\; f\,\hat\sigma_\text{LiDAR})\)
\(\boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{cc}\) (K=6) \(\mathcal{N}(\hat{\boldsymbol\mu}_\text{LiDAR},\; f\,\hat{\boldsymbol\sigma}_\text{LiDAR})\)
\(\boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{pct}\) (K=8) \(\mathcal{N}(\hat{\boldsymbol\mu}_\text{LiDAR},\; f\,\hat{\boldsymbol\sigma}_\text{LiDAR})\)
\(\delta\) \(\mathcal{N}(0, 1)\)
\(\theta_\text{mbw}\), \(\theta_\text{help}\) \(\text{Beta}(1, 1)\)

where \(f\) is the inflation factor controlling how tightly the prior constrains the posterior to the LiDAR estimate (see Section 8.3 for a sensitivity analysis over \(f \in \{1, 2, 4, 10\}\); the production model uses \(f = 2\)).

5 Model Fitting

All models were sampled with CmdStanR: 2 chains × 10,000 iterations (5,000 warmup), adapt_delta = 0.95.

5.1 Code (not evaluated — loads pre-computed)

# -- Main joint model (JOINT_HELP_PIPELINE.R) --
mod  <- cmdstan_model("production_run_joint_HELP/joint_HELP_model.stan")
fit  <- mod$sample(data = stan_data, chains = 2, parallel_chains = 2,
                   iter_warmup = 5000, iter_sampling = 5000, adapt_delta = 0.95)
fit$save_object("production_run_joint_HELP/models/joint_HELP_fit.rds")

# -- Uninformed comparison models (FIT_UNINFORMED_MODELS.R) --
# HELP-only: pass matrix(0, 0, K_cc) for X_cc_mbw; N_cc_mbw = 0L
# MBW-only:  pass matrix(0, 0, K_cc) for X_cc_help; N_cc_help = 0L
fit_help <- mod_flex$sample(data = stan_data_help, ...)
fit_mbw  <- mod_flex$sample(data = stan_data_mbw,  ...)

6 Data Source Comparison

We evaluate seven models spanning all non-null combinations of three information sources — L (LiDAR structural prior), M (MBW passive point-counts), and H (HELP playback surveys) — on the full combined MBW + HELP dataset.

Evaluation strategy: For observations in a model’s training set, we use PSIS-LOO (which penalizes for in-sample overfitting). For observations genuinely held out (e.g., HELP surveys for an MBW-only model), we use lppd from the posterior, which is the appropriate predictive score when those observations truly could not have influenced the fit. For the prior-only model (L alone), lppd is computed by drawing from the LiDAR structural posterior with Beta(1,1) hurdle probabilities and a Normal(0,1) HELP offset. All models are evaluated on the same N = 3991 observations, so ELPD values are directly comparable.

2³ factorial model comparison. ELPD evaluated on all MBW + HELP observations (N = combined dataset). ΔELPD relative to best model. For held-out protocol observations, lppd from the posterior is used in place of LOO.
Model LiDAR MBW HELP ELPD ΔELPD SE(ΔELPD)
MH ✓ ✓ -1367.3 0.0 0.0
LMH ✓ ✓ ✓ -1376.7 -9.4 4.7
M ✓ -1561.4 -194.1 17.3
LM ✓ ✓ -1598.9 -231.6 19.8
LH ✓ ✓ -2329.1 -961.9 34.2
H ✓ -2380.1 -1012.8 31.5
L ✓ -2542.9 -1175.6 37.4

The joint model combining all three data sources (LMH) achieved ELPD = -1377. The best-performing model was MH (ELPD = -1367), with the LiDAR prior slightly reducing out-of-sample accuracy relative to the flat-prior joint model (ΔELPD = -9, SE = 5). This is consistent with the sensitivity analysis (Section 8.3), where looser prior inflation (inf_factor = 10) progressively recovered performance toward the flat-prior baseline. MBW data contributed far more predictive power than HELP, as seen by comparing M vs H (ELPD -1561 vs -2380). The LiDAR prior alone (L, ELPD = -2543) performed worst, confirming that the structural habitat model does not substitute for direct survey data.

7 Main Results

7.1 Abundance chronosequence

7.2 Treatment effect

PCT treatment effect at selected stand ages.
TSSI (yr) TSCC (yr) CC abund PCT abund Ratio 95% CI Evidence
treatment_effect_mult[1] 0 12 0.328 0.322 1.00 [0.48, 1.82] ns
treatment_effect_mult[6] 5 17 0.362 0.378 1.05 [0.75, 1.44] ns
treatment_effect_mult[11] 10 22 0.340 0.504 1.49 [1.24, 1.76] pos **
treatment_effect_mult[16] 15 27 0.288 0.495 1.73 [1.29, 2.27] pos **
treatment_effect_mult[21] 20 32 0.260 0.541 2.12 [1.21, 3.42] pos **
treatment_effect_mult[26] 25 37 0.246 0.336 1.42 [0.58, 2.94] ns
treatment_effect_mult[31] 30 42 0.238 0.199 0.90 [0.23, 2.48] ns

The PCT treatment effect peaks near TSSI = 20 years (TSCC ≈ 32) with a posterior mean ratio of 2.12 (95% CI [1.21, 3.42]), indicating that thinned stands support approximately 2.12× the BITH abundance of untreated stands at the same forest age.

8 Prior Sensitivity and Supplementary Models

8.1 LiDAR prior (before survey data)

The prior predictive distribution shows what the LiDAR structural models predict before any point-count or playback data are incorporated. Curves represent the abundance shape inferred from NB stand structural scores alone.

8.2 Uninformed models (no LiDAR prior)

These models use weakly informative priors (Normal(0, 1) on spline coefficients; intercept centered on the log mean nonzero count with SD = 2) rather than the LiDAR-informed prior, showing what the survey data recover with minimal structural constraint. Three variants are compared: HELP playback data only, MBW passive count data only, and both protocols jointly. Point estimates use the posterior median.

8.3 Prior inflation sensitivity (inf_factor)

The LiDAR posterior SDs are multiplied by inf_factor before being passed to the Stan model as prior standard deviations. This controls how tightly the survey data is constrained to the LiDAR estimate — a larger factor allows the survey data more freedom to update the abundance curve shape. The production model uses inf_factor = 2, but this choice was heuristic. Here we assess sensitivity across inf_factor ∈ {1, 2, 4, 10}.

Key parameter posteriors across inf_factor values.
